content.text: is a hardware setup that uses multiple graphics processing units (GPUs) together to handle complex computational tasks, enabling faster training and inference of large deep learning models.
By default, deep learning models only utilize a
for training, even if multiple GPUs are available. Multi-GPU training distributes the workload across all available GPUs to
and enable training on more data.
Why Multi-GPU Matters in Generative AI
Multi-GPU training is essential for modern AI models:
Reduce training time by distributing data across GPUs
Train models that don't fit on single GPU (e.g., 100B+ parameter LLMs)
Process massive batches that exceed single GPU memory
Enable faster application development and more experimentation
Achieve up to 10x training performance improvements
The two fundamental approaches to multi-GPU training:
: Different parts (layers) of the model are placed on different GPUs

Model (Too Large for Single GPU)
├── Layer 1 → GPU 0
├── Layer 2 → GPU 1
├── Layer 3 → GPU 2
└── Layer 4 → GPU 3

that don't fit on a single GPU
- Models with billions of parameters (e.g., GPT-3, LLaMA)

:
- Enables training models that exceed single GPU memory

: Requires data flow between GPUs when activations transfer
-
when data propagates through layers on 2nd GPU
: Replicate the model across all GPUs, divide data into batches, each GPU processes its batch

Data Parallelism
├── GPU 0: Model → Batch 1 → Gradients
├── GPU 1: Model → Batch 2 → Gradients
├── GPU 2: Model → Batch 3 → Gradients
├── GPU 3: Model → Batch 4 → Gradients
└── All GPUs: Aggregate Gradients → Update Model

process
- Most common approach for deep learning
: Just replicate model and split data

:
- Limited by single GPU memory for model size
: Distributes individual tensor operations (e.g., matrix multiplication) across multiple devices

Large Matrix Multiplication
├── Split matrix into smaller parts
├── GPU 0: Compute part 1
├── GPU 1: Compute part 2
├── GPU 2: Compute part 3
└── GPU 3: Compute part 4 → Combine results

:
- Large tensor operations that can be divided
- Inherently built into PyTorch/TensorFlow
: Combination of data parallelism + model parallelism

Pipeline Parallelism (Improves GPU Utilization)
t=1: GPU 0 processes micro-batch 1 → GPU 1 idle
t=2: GPU 1 processes micro-batch 1 → GPU 0 processes micro-batch 2
t=3: GPU 2 processes micro-batch 1 → GPU 1 processes micro-batch 2 → GPU 0 processes micro-batch 3

receives activations from 1st micro-batch

import torch
import torch.nn as nn
from torch.nn import DataParallel

# Model
model = MyModel()

# Wrap model for multi-GPU
model = DataParallel(model, device_ids=[0, 1, 2, 3])  # Use GPUs 0-3

# Training (automatic)
input = torch.randn(128, 784)  # Batch split across GPUs
output = model(input)  # Processes on all GPUs
loss = compute_loss(output, target)
loss.backward()
optimizer.step()
for multiple GPUs on the same node

import torch
import torch.distributed as dist
import torch.nn.parallel as nn

# Initialize distributed training
dist.init_process_group("nccl")  # NCCL backend for GPUs

# Model
model = MyModel()

# Wrap for distributed training
model = nn.DistributedDataParallel(model, device_ids=[0, 1, 2, 3])

# Training (each GPU gets different batch)
input = torch.randn(32, 784)  # 32 per GPU (128 total / 4 GPUs)
output = model(input)
loss = compute_loss(output, target)
loss.backward()
optimizer.step()

import tensorflow as tf

# MirroredStrategy for multi-GPU training on single node
strategy = tf.distribute.MirroredStrategy()  # Automatic multi-GPU [web:243]

# Create model within strategy
with strategy.scope():
    model = tf.keras.Sequential([
        tf.keras.layers.Dense(256, activation='relu', input_shape=(784,)),
        tf.keras.layers.Dense(10)
    ])
    
    model.compile(
        optimizer='adam',
        loss='mse'
    )

# Training (automatic distribution)
model.fit(train_data, train_labels, epochs=10)  # Auto distributes to all GPUs

: Use NCCL (NVIDIA Collective Communications Library) for optimized GPU communication

call is enough to start using GPU
Single GPU → Multi-GPU Workstation (2-4 GPUs) → Multi-GPU Server (8-10 GPUs) → Cluster (100+ GPUs)
: Batch size should be divisible by number of GPUs
: 128 total batch on 4 GPUs = 32 per GPU
: Split one huge task among workers (each handles part)
: Each worker has same task, processes different data
: Workers form a pipeline, passing work to each processor
: Split one big calculation among workers

The theoretical speedup from N GPUs is bounded by Amdahl's law. If a fraction p of training is parallelisable, the maximum speedup is 1 / (1 - p + p/N). In practice, communication overhead from gradient synchronisation prevents perfect scaling. With all-reduce collective operations, each GPU sends and receives O(P) bytes per step, where P is the number of model parameters.

For ring all-reduce — the standard algorithm used by NCCL — each GPU participates in two phases (reduce-scatter then all-gather), each transferring 2(N-1)/N × P bytes total. The communication time is:

A 7B-parameter model in float32 requires 28 GB of gradient data per all-reduce. Over NVLink (600 GB/s), this takes ~47 ms. Over PCIe 4.0 (32 GB/s), it takes ~875 ms. A forward+backward pass on A100 takes ~200 ms for a typical batch, so NVLink achieves near-linear scaling while PCIe does not. This is why intranode NVLink GPUs (DGX systems) achieve near-linear scaling but multi-node Ethernet clusters do not.
Model fits on 1 GPU, want faster training
Minimal communication overhead; near-linear scaling on NVLink
Model too large for single GPU
Only way to fit weights + activations
Very deep model, GPU utilisation low
Overlaps compute stages to reduce idle time
Splits the matrix op itself across GPUs
Shards optimizer state + gradients across all ranks
Combines all three to saturate thousands of GPUs

Do NOT use multi-GPU when: the bottleneck is data loading (fix the DataLoader first), when the model is small and fits easily on one GPU (communication overhead dominates), or when gradient synchronisation requires very unequal batch sizes across GPUs (causes load imbalance).
The recommended production approach for data parallelism. Each process owns one GPU and communicates gradients via NCCL all-reduce.

# train_ddp.py — launch with: torchrun --nproc_per_node=4 train_ddp.py
import os
import torch
import torch.nn as nn
import torch.distributed as dist
from torch.nn.parallel import DistributedDataParallel as DDP
from torch.utils.data import DataLoader, TensorDataset
from torch.utils.data.distributed import DistributedSampler

def main():
    # torchrun sets RANK, LOCAL_RANK, WORLD_SIZE automatically
    dist.init_process_group(backend="nccl")
    rank       = dist.get_rank()
    local_rank = int(os.environ["LOCAL_RANK"])
    world_size = dist.get_world_size()
    device     = torch.device(f"cuda:{local_rank}")

    # Each process constructs and wraps the model independently
    model = nn.Sequential(
        nn.Linear(784, 512), nn.ReLU(),
        nn.Linear(512, 10)
    ).to(device)
    model = DDP(model, device_ids=[local_rank])

    # DistributedSampler ensures each rank sees a disjoint data subset
    dataset = TensorDataset(
        torch.randn(1024, 784), torch.randint(0, 10, (1024,))
    )
    sampler    = DistributedSampler(dataset, num_replicas=world_size, rank=rank)
    dataloader = DataLoader(dataset, batch_size=32, sampler=sampler)

    optimizer = torch.optim.Adam(model.parameters(), lr=1e-3)
    loss_fn   = nn.CrossEntropyLoss()

    for epoch in range(3):
        sampler.set_epoch(epoch)  # Crucial: re-shuffles data differently each epoch
        for x, y in dataloader:
            x, y = x.to(device), y.to(device)
            optimizer.zero_grad()
            loss = loss_fn(model(x), y)
            loss.backward()   # All-reduce happens here automatically
            optimizer.step()
        if rank == 0:
            print(f"Epoch {epoch}: loss={loss.item():.4f}")

    dist.destroy_process_group()

if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()
Example 2: Fully Sharded Data Parallel (FSDP) for Large Models

FSDP (PyTorch's ZeRO-3 equivalent) shards model parameters, gradients, and optimizer state across all ranks. This lets you train a model whose total size exceeds any single GPU's memory by a factor of N.

# fsdp_example.py — launch with: torchrun --nproc_per_node=4 fsdp_example.py
import os
import torch
import torch.nn as nn
import torch.distributed as dist
from torch.distributed.fsdp import FullyShardedDataParallel as FSDP
from torch.distributed.fsdp.wrap import size_based_auto_wrap_policy
import functools

class LargeTransformerBlock(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, d_model=4096, nhead=32):
        super().__init__()
        self.attn = nn.MultiheadAttention(d_model, nhead, batch_first=True)
        self.ff   = nn.Sequential(
            nn.Linear(d_model, d_model * 4),
            nn.GELU(),
            nn.Linear(d_model * 4, d_model)
        )
        self.norm1 = nn.LayerNorm(d_model)
        self.norm2 = nn.LayerNorm(d_model)

    def forward(self, x):
        x = self.norm1(x + self.attn(x, x, x)[0])
        x = self.norm2(x + self.ff(x))
        return x

def main():
    dist.init_process_group(backend="nccl")
    local_rank = int(os.environ["LOCAL_RANK"])
    device = torch.device(f"cuda:{local_rank}")
    torch.cuda.set_device(device)

    # Wrap policy: shard any submodule with ≥100M parameters
    wrap_policy = functools.partial(size_based_auto_wrap_policy, min_num_params=100_000_000)

    model = nn.Sequential(*[LargeTransformerBlock() for _ in range(8)])
    model = FSDP(model, auto_wrap_policy=wrap_policy, device_id=local_rank)

    optimizer = torch.optim.AdamW(model.parameters(), lr=1e-4)

    # Training step
    x = torch.randn(2, 128, 4096, device=device)
    loss = model(x).mean()
    loss.backward()
    optimizer.step()

    if dist.get_rank() == 0:
        print(f"FSDP training step completed, loss={loss.item():.4f}")

    dist.destroy_process_group()

if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()
Real-World Example: Training a Vision Transformer on ImageNet
Meta's training of ViT-H/14 (632M parameters) on ImageNet-21k used 256 A100 GPUs across 32 nodes with DDP + gradient checkpointing. The key production setup:

# Production DDP training recipe (simplified from common practice)
import torch
from torch.nn.parallel import DistributedDataParallel as DDP
from torch.cuda.amp import GradScaler, autocast

def production_training_step(model, batch, optimizer, scaler, device):
    """
    Production training step with:
    - Mixed precision (fp16) to reduce memory and double throughput
    - Gradient scaling to avoid fp16 underflow
    - Gradient clipping to prevent explosion during all-reduce
    """
    x, y = batch
    x, y = x.to(device, non_blocking=True), y.to(device, non_blocking=True)

    optimizer.zero_grad(set_to_none=True)  # Faster than zero_grad()

    with autocast(dtype=torch.float16):    # Forward in fp16
        logits = model(x)
        loss   = torch.nn.functional.cross_entropy(logits, y)

    scaler.scale(loss).backward()          # Scale before backward
    scaler.unscale_(optimizer)             # Unscale before clip
    torch.nn.utils.clip_grad_norm_(model.parameters(), max_norm=1.0)
    scaler.step(optimizer)
    scaler.update()

    return loss.item()

# Setup:
# model = DDP(ViT_H_14().to(device), device_ids=[local_rank])
# scaler = GradScaler()
# optimizer = torch.optim.AdamW(model.parameters(), lr=1e-3, weight_decay=0.1)
# Global batch size = 4096 across 256 GPUs = 16 per GPU
# 90k steps × ~1.1s/step on A100 = ~28 GPU-hours per GPU = 7168 total GPU-hours
This setup achieved 85.2% top-1 ImageNet accuracy. Without multi-GPU, training at this scale would take 180,000 GPU-hours on a single A100 — 25× longer due to the reduced effective batch size.
model.zero_grad() sets gradients to zero tensors (keeping memory allocated). set_to_none=True deallocates gradient tensors, saving peak memory between steps.
Without this call, every epoch sees the same data order on every rank. The sampler uses epoch as a seed for shuffling — without updating it, all shuffled epochs are identical.
Using DataParallel instead of DDP for any real training.

DataParallel uses a GIL-bound Python thread per GPU and scatters/gathers tensors on GPU:0, creating a severe bottleneck at GPU:0. DDP uses a separate process per GPU with NCCL all-reduce. DDP is always faster for more than one GPU.
Not accounting for effective learning rate scaling.

With N GPUs and data parallelism, each gradient step uses N× more data than single-GPU training. A common rule (linear scaling rule, Goyal et al. 2017): scale the learning rate by N when multiplying batch size by N. Alternatively use a warmup schedule.
Logging or saving from all ranks instead of rank 0 only.

All N processes are running identical code. Without guarding with `if dist.get_rank() == 0:`, checkpoint saves and metric logs will be written N times, corrupting files or producing duplicate tensorboard entries.
GPU scaling efficiency degrades as the computation-to-communication ratio decreases. Larger batches and larger models improve this ratio. Key metrics for evaluating a multi-GPU setup:
Profile with torch.profiler, look for nccl ops
Same loss curve as single-GPU at same total examples

Gradient checkpointing (torch.utils.checkpoint.checkpoint) trades compute for memory by recomputing activations during the backward pass instead of storing them. It reduces activation memory by O(√layers) at the cost of ~33% more compute — a good trade for memory-bound workloads.
— the standard launcher for DDP and FSDP. Automatically sets RANK, LOCAL_RANK, WORLD_SIZE env vars. Replaces the older torch.multiprocessing.spawn API.
— wraps DDP/FSDP/DeepSpeed with a unified API. One accelerator.backward(loss) call handles gradient sync, mixed precision, and gradient accumulation transparently.
— Microsoft's optimizer that shards parameter states (ZeRO-1), gradients (ZeRO-2), or full parameters (ZeRO-3) across ranks. Enables trillion-parameter model training.
— implements 3D parallelism (tensor + pipeline + data) for transformer LLMs. Used by Megatron-Turing NLG and Llama internal training variants.
— the built-in PyTorch profiler; set record_shapes=True and profile_memory=True to see per-op memory and NCCL communication time breakdowns in TensorBoard.
Models fitting on 1 GPU; most common case
Huge attention heads or FFN layers
Very deep models; combine with DP
Legacy; pipeline PP is strictly better

When multiple parties contribute gradient updates (federated learning), a malicious participant can submit crafted gradients that steer the global model toward a backdoor. Defences include differential privacy (gradient clipping + noise), coordinate-wise median aggregation, and cosine similarity filtering of gradient updates.

Each distributed training job must use a unique rendezvous endpoint (MASTER_ADDR + MASTER_PORT). Shared ports allow other jobs to inject rogue ranks into an existing process group. Always use dynamically assigned ports and firewall training nodes from public networks.

Model checkpoints saved during multi-GPU training are loaded by all ranks at the start of resumed training. A tampered checkpoint silently corrupts all GPUs simultaneously. Verify checkpoint hashes (SHA-256) before loading and store checkpoints on authenticated storage.

In DDP, if one rank runs out of GPU memory and raises a CUDA OOM error, other ranks will hang indefinitely waiting for the all-reduce to complete. Set NCCL_ASYNC_ERROR_HANDLING=1 to detect and propagate errors across all ranks within a timeout.
